name: Sanitation and Clean Water
tagline: Safe drinking water, waste isolation, and hygiene habits that prevent waterborne disease when infrastructure disappears
problem:
Human beings are walking conflict zones for microbes: every litre of surface water near settlements is a potential culture medium seeded by faeces, urine, decaying biomass, and the invisible films called biofilms that protect pathogens from simple fixes. In the first hours after disruption, people still behave as if taps and chlorine exist; within days, diarrhoeal disease can remove more working adults from a labour pool than injury or violence. Children dehydrate fastest; caregivers then face impossible trade-offs between fetching water and staying at a bedside.

The second failure mode is engineering without discipline. A community can build an impressive filter or a gleaming boiling kettle and still poison itself by dipping unwashed hands into storage, by rinsing dishes in untreated stream water, or by locating a latrine uphill of the spring because the downhill slope was rockier to dig. Water safety is a system: source selection, transport, treatment, storage, and waste must be designed as one loop, not five independent hobbies.

The third failure mode is chemical complacency. Boiling does not remove arsenic, nitrates from agricultural runoff, or many industrial solvents. Clear water is not innocent water. Teaching people to recognise the limits of each method — heat, sand, sunlight, measured halogens — is as important as teaching them to tie knots.

Collapse and frontier habitats share one brutal truth: there is no invisible municipal labour force keeping the loop closed. Either your group maintains the loop consciously, or the loop closes you.
overview:
Practical field water treatment stacks independent barriers. First, protect the source: choose headwater springs or rain from clean roofs where possible; fence livestock away; avoid stirring sediment during collection. Second, reduce turbidity: settle in a series of jars or a drum, decanting progressively cleaner supernatant; pre-filter through cloth, then through packed sand and gravel when materials allow.

Third, disinfect: a rolling boil for one minute (longer at altitude) inactivates most bacterial and viral threats if the entire volume reaches temperature — thin-walled metal kettles transfer heat faster than thick pottery. Solar disinfection (SODIS) combines ultraviolet exposure and modest heating in clear bottles laid in full sun; throughput is low and weather-dependent but costs almost no fuel. Where halogen chemistry exists, measured contact time beats guesswork; under-dosing selects hardier survivors while overdosing tastes foul and can irritate mucosa.

Sanitation is the mirror image of drinking water: isolate human waste from fingers, flies, feet, and wind. Deep pit latrines with fly-proof covers, trench latrines rotated on a schedule, and meticulous separation of greywater from potable storage break the faecal-oral routes that drive cholera, shigellosis, and many helminths. Hand hygiene with soap removes a staggering fraction of risk; where soap is absent, ash paste and friction still outperform hope.

On Mars or in closed habitats, the physics changes form but not logic: every litre is inventoried, every waste stream is processed, and cross-contamination is an existential engineering fault rather than a regrettable statistic.

principles.name: Multi-barrier protection
principles.explanation: Settling, filtration, disinfection, and safe storage each remove different failure classes; redundancy beats optimism.

principles.name: Faecal-oral interruption
principles.explanation: Most epidemic diarrhoea is broken by distance, time, heat, chemistry, or hygiene that keeps pathogens from mouth.

principles.name: Turbidity and disinfection
principles.explanation: Particles shield microbes from UV and consume oxidants; clarify before final steps when possible.

principles.name: Hydraulic gradient
principles.explanation: Groundwater and surface flows follow gravity; latrines belong where leakage cannot reach intakes.

components.name: Protected intake
components.function: Collects water with minimal sediment disturbance
components.madeFrom: Screened pipe, bamboo, or ceramic conduit

components.name: Settling vessels
components.function: Gravity separation of sand and organic floc
components.madeFrom: Matched jars or drums with pour spouts

components.name: Sand and gravel filter
components.function: Removes particles; mature slow-sand bed hosts biocidal biofilm
components.madeFrom: Washed sand, gravel support, outlet valve
components.criticalNote: First litres after idle can be stagnant — discard

components.name: Boiling kettle
components.criticalNote: Steam burns; lid directs drips away from hands

components.name: Pit latrine with cover
components.criticalNote: Watch water table in wet season

rawMaterials.name: Source water
earthLocations: Rain from metal or tile roofs
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Mars: drill or trench for ice-rich regolith; sublimate or melt in closed processor; recycle condensate in habitat loops with brine management
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Assess dissolved salts and metals; blend or reject sources that fail simple taste and settle tests

rawMaterials.name: Filter media
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Sintered metal or ceramic cartridges imported until regolith washing lines exist
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Wash until rinse clears; sterilise new media with boiling water before commissioning

rawMaterials.name: Disinfectant or fuel
rawMaterials.purpose: Final kill step or heat source
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Electrolytic chlorine generators or UV LEDs with reliable power budgets
rawMaterials.processingRequired: If using halogens, measure mass per litre from manufacturer tables adapted to temperature

buildSteps.title: Walk the watershed before trusting any blue line on a map
buildSteps.description: Stand at the proposed intake during dry weather and again after rain. Note animal paths, burial sites, erosion, and upstream farms. Mark a fetch route that avoids dragging containers through mud that will foul rims.
buildSteps.warningNote: Industrial or mining districts may carry dissolved toxins boiling cannot remove — seek local knowledge

buildSteps.title: Build the decant chain before you are thirsty
buildSteps.description: Label three vessels: raw, settled, filtered. Move water only by decanting clear supernatant. Never pour dregs forward. Teach the sequence as a chant children can supervise.
buildSteps.warningNote: Heavy water jars cause crush injuries — knee-bend lifting or pair carry

buildSteps.title: Boil with a visible rolling boil
buildSteps.description: Whole volume must bubble, not just edges. Time one minute after full roll at low altitude; add time per public-health tables if you live high. Cool with lid cracked to limit airborne fallout.
buildSteps.warningNote: Open flame and steam scald — secure loose clothing and hair

buildSteps.title: Commission slow sand only with a fallback
buildSteps.description: Pack washed sand over gravel, wet slowly, and run discard water for days while still boiling drinking water. Measure flow so surface does not dry crack.
buildSteps.warningNote: Dry sand dust damages lungs — wet packing only

buildSteps.title: Dig latrine first class, not last minute
buildSteps.description: Depth below local water table guidance, offset from springs, fly-proof seat, daily ash cover, separate tool for lime if used. Post a handwash procedure at the path.
buildSteps.warningNote: Trench walls can collapse in loose soil — never dig alone

history.event: Cisterns, qanats, and aqueducts distribute water across dry civilisations.
Cholera outbreak mapping links water source to disease — modern field epidemiology begins.
Chlorination and rapid sand filtration drive down typhoid in piped cities.

impact:
Communities that keep drinking water and faeces apart survive long enough to argue about laws, calendars, and machines. Communities that fail this lesson disappear quietly into fever charts.

Space habitats inherit the same moral: there is no dilution sink — only engineering and procedure.
